active_ingredients: Glimepiride 1 mg, 2 mg, 4 mg tablets (e.g., Amaryl) - prescription only
indications_and_usage: Indicated as an adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus; may be used alone or combined with metformin or insulin. It is a second-generation sulfonylurea. Prescription only.
contraindications: Glimepiride is contraindicated in patients with hypersensitivity to glimepiride or any of the product's ingredients, and in patients with a history of allergic reaction to sulfonamide derivatives - cross-sensitivity is possible; type 1 diabetes; diabetic ketoacidosis.
warnings_and_cautions: Hypoglycemia - higher risk in the elderly, debilitated/malnourished patients, adrenal/pituitary insufficiency, renal or hepatic impairment, missed meals, alcohol, strenuous exercise, combined antidiabetic therapy; symptoms can be masked by beta-blockers. Sulfonamide cross-allergy as above. Hemolytic anemia in G6PD deficiency. Weight gain. Reduced effectiveness over time. Macrovascular outcomes - inform patients.
adverse_reactions: Hypoglycemia, headache, dizziness, nausea, weakness, weight gain, rash; rarely jaundice, hyponatremia, hematologic abnormalities.
drug_interactions: Drugs increasing hypoglycemic effect - other antidiabetic agents, NSAIDs, sulfonamides, fluconazole, fibrates, MAO inhibitors, beta-blockers (mask symptoms), salicylates, alcohol. Drugs decreasing effect - thiazides, corticosteroids, sympathomimetics, phenytoin, niacin, oral contraceptives. CYP2C9 inhibitors/inducers (gemfibrozil increases AUC; rifampin lowers it).
dosage_and_administration: Adults: start 1-2 mg once daily with the first main meal (typically breakfast); titrate every 1-2 weeks by 1-2 mg increments based on glucose response and tolerability; usual maintenance 1-4 mg once daily; maximum 8 mg once daily. In elderly, debilitated/malnourished, or renal/hepatic impairment, start at 1 mg daily and titrate cautiously. Always take with a meal to reduce hypoglycemia risk.
overdosage: Severe prolonged hypoglycemia (sometimes lasting days); treat with IV dextrose and close glucose monitoring; carbohydrate intake; admit if needed. Seek emergency care; call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222).
